content:
Supervised **loss functions** emerged from two parallel lines: **least squares** for continuous errors and **log-likelihood** for discrete outcomes.

**Gauss** and **Legendre** developed least squares in the early 1800s — minimizing squared residuals became the foundation for **MSE** in regression.

**Laplace** favored absolute errors (**MAE**), noting different sensitivity to outliers — a trade-off still debated today.

In the 20th century, **logistic regression** adopted **log loss** (binary cross-entropy) as the natural objective for Bernoulli models.

**Shannon's information theory** (1948) and later **Kullback-Leibler** work formalized **cross-entropy H(P, Q)** — the theoretical home for CE and BCE (see **Cross-entropy** topic).

Modern deep learning standardized **softmax + CE** for multi-class nets and **sigmoid + BCE** for binary heads — the same four losses at scale in classical ML and neural networks.

timeline.label: Least squares (1800s)
timeline.description: Gauss and Legendre establish squared-error minimization for fitting curves — origin of MSE as a training objective.

timeline.label: Logistic regression (mid-1900s)
timeline.description: Log loss / BCE becomes the standard objective for binary probabilistic classification.

timeline.label: Information theory (1948+)
timeline.description: Shannon entropy and cross-entropy H(P, Q) unify classification loss with expected code length — detailed in Cross-entropy topic.

timeline.label: Deep learning era
timeline.description: Framework losses (MSELoss, BCEWithLogitsLoss, CrossEntropyLoss) make the four canonical supervised losses defaults across sklearn, PyTorch, and TensorFlow.
